title: Add name-generation test
body:
Podman's container-name generation depends on the global RNG state being properly initialized (seeded).  Should this not happen for some reason (or it's seeded with a static value), podman will generate the exact same repeating sequence of container names (assuming no clashes with existing containers).  Add a test to confirm this is always the case.

<!--

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

comments.body:
> such as a top-level comment

Oh, great point. Yes please.

> Oh, great point. Yes please.

Isn't this the purpose of the `It("blah")` line?  Looking through the e2e tests, basically none of them have a top-level comment (or many comments at all for that matter) :disappointed: So...errr...at least the new test is consistent :rofl: 

I did put a good description in the commit, which git-blame will point people directly at.  That's got to be worth something :grin:
My fault for not reading that, that’s indeed a good place.

Thing is, most tests make sense. This one is baffling: as a human reader, it seems completely pointless. When something seems pointless, sometimes it _is_ (leftovers from prior era), sometimes it _isn't_ (very good reason, not obvious at first sight). Nobody wants to di `git blame`. As a maintainer, if someone forces me to do that, I'm going to resent them. We have a pretty neat technology, in which a developer can write explanations right next to the actual code, without incurring a compile-time cost, nor run-time, nor code bloat. It's something that takes a few seconds to add, and can save future maintainers many minutes of headscratching.

But, shrug, I can add the comments in the future when we do ginkgov2 and the Flake thing. (I'll resent it, though).

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *cevich*, *edsantiago*, *Luap99* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99,edsantiago] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

Force-push: Attempt to fix empty `cid` value from [previous test failure](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/6354557333143552/html/int-podman-fedora-38-root-host-sqlite.log.html#t--podman-generates-different-names-for-successive-containers--1).

/lgtm

If we start seeing flakes, one possible fix is to run ~ten creates, store results in a map, and `Expect(numberofkeys).To(<3)`

> If we start seeing flakes

@mtrmac or @Luap99 follow-up to the flake concern:  Is there anything else besides names that uses the global RNG?  Like container or image IDs or IP addresses, something with a larger pool of choices?

>  Is there anything else besides names that uses the global RNG? Like container or image IDs or IP addresses, something with a larger pool of choices?

I didn’t notice anything obviously directly user-visible in a quick skim of `git grep -F rand. -- $(git grep -l math/rand)` on the Podman repo.

---

An extreme option would be to add a hidden Podman command (to the primary binary that we otherwise test, not a small helper utility, to trigger the same `init()`s) that just generates a 64-bit number, prints it, and and exits. That doesn’t seem worthwhile.

> That doesn’t seem worthwhile.

I agree, but thanks for taking a look and suggesting it.  It's probably a good thing only the name-generator depends on the global state anyway.  Thanks @mtrmac
